notes: IBM 3090-180J cited at $8M 5-year lifecycle cost; ~40 TPS with CICS/DB2. Replaced by System 390/zSeries.
Proposed commercial OLTP system based on 32-way UNIX SMP. Never achieved commercial success.
IBM Customer Information Control System; dominant TP monitor for mainframe OLTP.
IBM's mainframe relational database; performance limitations cited for decision support workloads at time.
De facto OLTP benchmark; measures TPS at 95th percentile <1 second response time.
Cited as highly desirable for military/government OLTP; key to Encore's commercial strategy.
200 TPS per IBM 3090-200; peak 2,000 TPS; growing at 15% per annum.
Aberdeen predicted insurance industry would embrace digital imaging with OODB.

lifecycle_current: active as IBM i on Power Systems
notes: Aberdeen rated AS/400 as immature in 1989: lacking C compiler, 1MB program limit, journaling reduces TPS by up to 60%.
HP 3000 Models 48, 70, 925LX, 935, 950, 955, 960 benchmarked. HP ended support December 31, 2010.
RISC architecture; Models 935, 950, 955, 960. HP 950 recommended as entry point for Stanley-Vidmar.
Aberdeen normalized DebitCredit across vendors; used to compare HP and AS/400 TPS performance.
Proprietary IBM benchmark; Aberdeen and Arthur Andersen found RAMP-C methodology suspect for HP comparison.
HP's hierarchical database; fastest HP file system for OLTP. Aberdeen expects 20% HPPA perf improvement in 1990.
HP relational database; Aberdeen expects 50% performance improvement in 1990.

tech_name: AS/400 SQL/400 and OS/400 Native File System
lifecycle_current: evolved into IBM i Db2 for i
notes: Single-level storage; Aberdeen cited crash recovery as riskier than conventional midrange approach.
AS/400 manufacturing ERP package; local distributor support seen as risk factor for Stanley-Vidmar.
Aberdeen argued MIPS is invalid for comparing AS/400 and HPPA due to multiprocessor architecture differences.
v5 for decision support; v6 first designed for high-performance OLTP. Aberdeen recommends v6.3 for Bull.

lifecycle_current: evolved into SAP Adaptive Server Enterprise; acquired by SAP 2010
notes: v3 for decision support; v4 first designed for high-performance OLTP. Reliability issues noted vs. Ingres.
Rejected by virtually all contacts for architectural weaknesses for high-performance OLTP.
ANSI SQL-86/SQL-89 standard; QUEL (Ingres query language) being replaced by SQL in Ingres.
Aberdeen notes customers needed outside consulting help to understand client-server; adoption cycle ~2-3 years.
Customers looking forward to joint Cadre/Ingres CASE tools; Knowledge Manager seen as growth path.
Trade press reported Salomon Brothers might move IMS applications to Sybase; survey found Salomon wished it had used Ingres.
Aberdeen noted Sybase pro-actively supporting only Sun Microsystems and PS/2-OS/2 platforms.
Subject of study; enterprise perception: toy computer, lacks applications, connectivity myths.

tech_name: IBM PS/2 and IBM PC Architecture
notes: Over 80% of corporate MIS committed to IBM PC; PS/2 Model 55SX and up cited as client platform.
Most frequently mentioned target for client applications; has established itself as a standard.
Used for multithreading or IBM OfficeVision/2 commitments; described as 'late and slow' by MIS.
Dominant LAN OS; Macintosh support for Netware 286 exists but unknown to MIS; Netware 386 Mac support delayed.
Installed base ~5 million; declining at 15-20% per year through 1993; PC replacement underway.

lifecycle_current: market niche; subsumed by client-server and web
notes: Aberdeen estimates frontware market at under $50M in 1990; IBM blessed Easel Corp as frontware-of-choice.
95%+ of mainframe-size applications still using dumb 3270 terminals; maturity projected for mid-1994.
Departmental communications gateway topology emerging; Aberdeen forecasts rapid growth.
IBM SAA for cooperative processing from PS/2s to CICS/DB2 still 2 years away in 1991; weak customer commitment seen.
IBM-blessed frontware and client development toolset; most frequently mentioned by MIS sites.
Apple-promoted frontware product; only UT Austin using in production; criticized as too complex.

lifecycle_current: evolved; IBM acquired Software AG integration portfolio 2023
notes: Macintosh-supported client CASE tool and 4GL for mainframe host; used by UT Austin.
Native APPC applications planned at Merrill Lynch for 1991; Merrill Lynch expects common use by 1994.
Non-starters for corporate MIS desktops but Unix servers attracting interest for RDBMS decision support.
Aberdeen-coined marketing term for Encore's Dirty Computing; combination of SMP + clustering + memory-coupling + single-system view + high availability
Key differentiator enabling cache-coherent shared memory across cluster nodes without message-passing overhead
Standard technique for multi-CPU systems; now ubiquitous in all server and desktop processors
Evolved into IBM zSeries (2000) then IBM z Systems; primary incumbent competitor in financial services
Primary RDBMS running on S/390 mainframes; still active as IBM Db2 across multiple platforms
10+ year incumbent in loosely-coupled clustering; DEC acquired by Compaq 1998, VAXcluster discontinued
Recommended as RDBMS partner; Oracle 6.2 cited; now Oracle Database still dominant
Recommended as primary RDBMS partner; ASK acquired by Computer Associates 1994; Ingres open-sourced 2004
Tandem acquired by Compaq 1997; now HP NonStop still active in financial/payment processing
Used by Data General and Encore Series 91/93; discontinued 1997; Motorola shifted to PowerPC
Hardware bus standard used in Encore systems; still used in defense/industrial embedded systems
C language and Unix cited as software investment protection in Encore systems; now Linux dominates but Unix lineage continues
Recommended performance demonstration target of 1000-2000 TPS; TPC-A retired 1995, replaced by TPC-C
Object-oriented 4GL for IBM mainframes; evolved to multi-platform SaaS insurance suite
Primary OS environment for Sapiens; evolved into OS/390 then z/OS
One of three IBM SAA environments Sapiens supports
One of three IBM SAA environments Sapiens supports
IBM's cross-platform portability architecture; Kastner notes it as 'still undelivered' in 1992; SAA largely failed
Sapiens builds its internal DBMS (DB1) on top of VSAM; VSAM still used in mainframe applications today
Primary 3GL competitor to Sapiens; Kastner cites 15-30% Sapiens performance penalty vs tuned COBOL
Kastner observes Sapiens 3270 screens compare poorly with Windows GUI; automatic conversion to Windows is a key differentiator
Windows client for Sapiens in pre-release form during Feb 1992 visit; Q2 1992 availability targeted
Sapiens R&D project to port runtime kernel from IBM assembler to C/C++ for Unix; strategic for migration path
Cited as platform with no market leader yet for cross-platform IBM mainframe + RS/6000 integration tools
Kastner describes Sapiens OO architecture as 'perhaps state-of-the-art' in 1992; encapsulation + message-passing
Primary benchmark analyzed in study; transaction processing successor to Debit/Credit; measures TPS-A with terminal connectivity; retired in 2001 replaced by TPC-C and TPC-E
Database-only benchmark successor to TP1; no terminal network component; favored by database and hot-box suppliers; retired in 2001
Order-entry mixed workload OLTP benchmark; under public review at time of study; Aberdeen predicted it would become very important — it did, becoming dominant OLTP benchmark
DEC's flagship CISC minicomputer platform; VAX 6000-640 achieved over 200 TPS-A; price-performance realigned March 15 1992; line discontinued after Compaq acquisition
DEC entry-to-midrange VAX; MicroVAX 3100-80 had best industry price-performance at $7.69K/TPS-A in March 1992; MicroVAX 4000-300 showed 3x improvement over 20 months
Proprietary OS for VAX and Alpha; described in study as CISC-based proprietary OS; continues today as OpenVMS maintained by VMS Software Inc.
DEC's relational database; key to VAX TPC-A performance improvements; software efficiency gains described as free hardware upgrade; now maintained by Oracle as Oracle Rdb
Peripheral Component Interconnect bus standard; emerging at time of study; became dominant PC/server bus standard; now largely superseded by PCIe
Unix-based server platforms from Bull, Sun, Sequent, Data General; gaining TPC-A competitiveness in 1992; Unix/Linux ultimately dominant in enterprise servers
Relational database management systems; virtually all TPC results used RDBMS; significant growth driver; now dominant paradigm for transactional data
IBM midrange platform; shown as having 77% price-performance gap between entry E10 and high-end E70; continues today as IBM i (Power Systems)
HP proprietary midrange system running MPE/iX; 61% price-performance and 171% performance improvement since Jan 1990; HP discontinued platform in 2008
HP proprietary OS for HP 3000 systems; shared Precision Architecture RISC processors with HP-UX; discontinued with HP 3000 in 2008
HP's Unix OS for HP 9000 and Integrity servers; shared PA-RISC architecture with MPE/iX; still supported by HPE but in maintenance mode
HP's relational database; improved from also-ran to serious contender status by 1992; eventually discontinued and replaced by other HP database offerings
DEC's next-generation 64-bit RISC processor architecture; still-to-be-announced at time of study; succeeded VAX; platform eventually discontinued after HP acquisition
Evolved to SAP ERP then SAP ECC then SAP S/4HANA; R/3 mainstream maintenance ended ~2027
Aberdeen's recommended platform; 45%+ RISC/Unix market share 1992; PA-RISC architecture through 7 generations; Oracle's primary Unix revenue platform
5th DEC Unix attempt in 5 years; Aberdeen found ISVs still considered it beta; no TPC-A results available at June 1993; DEC itself told Aberdeen OpenVMS was primary OS
15% RISC/Unix market share 1992; AIX 3.2 stability issues; RIOS processor end-of-life visible; successor PowerPC 600 not yet released
Stability reported poor; IBM created Toronto development center to improve; AIX/6000 successor expected before end 1993; AIX still active as AIX 7.x on IBM Power Systems
Top-tier Unix stability; HP led industry with OpenView extensions; HP-UX 11i still supported until end 2025
SAP R3 back-end database; Oracle uses HP 9000 as Unix reference port; Oracle SQL*NET for distributed connectivity
7 generations of PA-RISC by 1993; HP-UX TPC-A results highest in industry; succeeded by Intel Itanium in HP-UX servers
IBM's planned successor to RIOS; part of AIM alliance; not yet in production for multiuser systems in June 1993
DEC's stated primary OS for multiuser production computing (not OSF1); still maintained by VMS Software Inc.
HP 890-400 TPC-A results higher than IBM 3090-200; DEC Alpha/OSF1 TPC-A results not yet available June 1993
Hercules' existing mainframe to be replaced; IBM will not run TPC-A tests on 3090; target for mainframe elimination by Q1 1996
Standard for electronic business document exchange (purchase orders, invoices); paper positioned as core B2B enabler. Evolved into modern API-based B2B integration.
Paper identified Internet as ideal technology base for e-commerce; described as enabling a paradigm shift in buying and selling.
Internal corporate network connecting employees and workgroups; paper saw as essential for virtual corporation efficiency.
Decision support systems for anticipating demand in the supply chain; evolved into modern big data and business intelligence platforms.
Network security technology listed as key EC security component.
Listed as key EC security technology; became foundational via SSL/TLS for all web commerce.
Online credit card processing; became dominant Internet payment method, now supplemented by digital wallets.
Early concept of digital currency for Internet transactions; mostly failed in 1990s form (e.g., DigiCash) but concept re-emerged with cryptocurrency.
Paper noted EC adds new media capabilities onto existing database technology; RDBMS remains foundational.
Referenced as target for 4GL and CASE tool code generation; became dominant enterprise web technology.
Paper mentioned CGI replacements as middleware category; CGI was standard for dynamic web content, later replaced by application servers.
CORBA-style distributed object middleware; paper cited as key EC technology. Largely replaced by web services and REST APIs.
Listed as collaboration enabler for both B2B and B2C; remains core business communication tool.
Document management and workflow tools; evolved into modern BPM and enterprise content management.
Fourth-generation languages and computer-aided software engineering; paper saw generating Java. Largely replaced by modern IDEs and frameworks.
Payment and identity authentication; evolved into modern OAuth, multi-factor authentication, and identity management.
Distributed transaction processing component; managing state across HTTP sessions became fundamental to web applications.
Specialized e-commerce application development tools; evolved into platforms like Shopify, Magento, WooCommerce.
Core help desk/ITSM platform; AR System 3.0 released Nov 1996. Evolved into BMC Remedy ITSM Suite; still marketed by BMC as of 2024.
Aberdeen-coined category for unified internal IT operations automation; concept absorbed into ITSM and ERP categories by 2000s.
Category that spawned ITSM; Aberdeen saw help desk as launchpad for broader COM adoption.
Critical success factor in Aberdeen's COM framework; embedded in AR System v3.0.
Released November 1996; added HTML User Guide, view management, Direct SQL, improved workflow embedding.
High-density modular RAC installed in Bay 5000 MSX chassis; targeted at service providers, telcos, carriers, large enterprises. Featured BayDSP modem technology.

tech_name: Bay Networks Model 8000 Remote Access Concentrator
notes: Stand-alone RAC targeted at smaller ISPs and enterprise users; also features BayDSP modem technology from Penril acquisition.
Digital Signal Processing-based modem technology acquired via Penril Datability Networks; enabled 56K modem capabilities.
Competing standards x2 (3Com/US Robotics) and K56flex (Rockwell) were unified by ITU V.90 standard ratified September 1998. All dial-up modem tech obsolete post-broadband.
Modular chassis in which 5399 RAC installs; part of Bay's multi-service access product family.

tech_name: Bay Networks Switch Node Routing Switch
notes: Announced March 3, 1997. Layer 3 switching with dual 1.2 Gbps buses, IP AutoLearn, 5-slot chassis; wire-speed IP/IPX routing. Discontinued after Bay's acquisition by Nortel.
Bay proprietary feature; transparently integrates with existing routers without reconfiguration; automatically learns IP forwarding tables.
Combining IP/IPX routing with wire-speed hardware switching; became standard campus network design; evolved into modern multilayer switching.
Virtual circuit switching alternative to IP; Aberdeen cited as too complex for campus environments vs. Bay's switch-node approach.
Software foundation for Switch Node; same BayRS used in Bay Network routers; provided IP/IPX routing control.
2-port 100Base-TX switching module available in Switch Node hardware; Fast Ethernet becoming dominant campus backbone.

CORBA-based, object-oriented high-end dynamic web application platform; supported personalized content, CRM, knowledge management, e-commerce. Still exists as BroadVision Commerce Agility Suite in evolved form.
Low-end dynamic web server technology; Aberdeen notes poor scaling with large user loads and high database overhead.
BroadVision One-to-One is CORBA-compliant; enables scaling and integration with IBM and Oracle CORBA systems.
Aberdeen predicted smart cards would bridge internet and physical retail; chip cards now universal (EMV standard) but used differently than Aberdeen envisioned.
PointCast and similar 'push' tech; Aberdeen dismissed as 'automated pull' not true personalization; category essentially disappeared by 2000.
Aberdeen's three-tier category (CGI/ODBC/CORBA) accurately described market structure; category evolved into modern web application frameworks (Django, Ruby on Rails, Node.js, etc.).
Listed as BroadVision One-to-One supported database; also Sybase 10x/11x, Informix 7x, MS SQL Server 6x.
BroadVision combines OO middleware with TP monitor technology to handle intense internet loads; key architectural differentiation.

Full enterprise management suite; $1B+ annual revenue run rate by 1997; 7,000+ customers, 200+ ISV partners, 37 OS platforms. Evolved into CA Technologies Unified Infrastructure Management. Now part of Broadcom.

Free/bundled subset of Unicenter TNG announced July 14, 1997; includes auto-discovery, object repository, 2D/3D mapping, event management, scheduling, virus scanning. 10-minute installation. 20,000 CDs distributed at CA World.
Enables ISVs and sophisticated IT orgs to build custom application management solutions using Framework as foundation.

CA's primary competitor; Kastner notes TME requires remote-network-supplied configuration estimates and 'days spent in network and asset evaluation' vs. CA's 10-minute install. Evolved into IBM Tivoli suite.
HP OpenView was dominant network management platform; Aberdeen says HP 'has clearly lost momentum' in 1997; HP's own systems division endorsed Unicenter Framework as preferred management platform.
Framework uses SNMP agents for monitoring; referenced in Unicenter TNG auto-discovery and event management.

Kastner describes 3D mapping as 'animation and elements of virtual reality to create realistic floor plans indicating location of devices within the building'; considered breakthrough enterprise management UI in 1997.
Framework initially operates on Windows NT Server; Unix versions promised for later summer 1997. NT compared to Unicenter TNG's $1B revenue run rate in Kastner's analysis.

Cabletron's market-leading network management platform; uses Inductive Modeling Technology (IMT); object-oriented distributed client/server architecture; IP for OpenSystems; major engineering investment from 1990; became Aprisma Spectrum post-Cabletron breakup; acquired by Concord then CA Technologies. Still exists as CA Spectrum as of 2020.

Policy-based VLAN and virtual networking architecture; four-phase rollout from basic switching to policy-based management; includes SecureFast SmartSwitches, VLAN Manager, Virtual Network Server; RFC 2643 documented the operational model (1999); dissolved with Cabletron reorganization.

ASIC-based switching hardware; 10M packets/second forwarding rate; up to 16,000 MAC addresses; redundant power/cooling/links; RMON support; upgrade path from shared LAN to FDDI, ATM, Fast Ethernet, Gigabit Ethernet; non-blocking switch fabric with distributed processing.

Flagship modular hub/switch chassis; integrated packet- and cell-switched backplane; 4 Gbps Internal Network Bus (INB); up to 500 ports; 10M packets/second; supports Ethernet, Token Ring, FDDI, SNA, ATM, Gigabit Ethernet; original MMAC introduced 1988 and upgradeable through all technology transitions.

Remote access product family for carrier/service provider down to SOHO markets; supports ISDN, frame relay, analog/digital modems; planned ADSL capability with Motorola Semiconductor; based on Network Express and Netlink acquisitions; Intel i960-based; dissolved with Cabletron reorganization.

Virtual LAN technology enabling logical network segmentation on shared physical infrastructure; Aberdeen notes Cabletron started VLAN work in 1990 before IEEE 802.1Q standard; IEEE 802.1Q standardized 1998; VLANs remain fundamental to modern enterprise networking.

Aberdeen's coined concept for emerging market where LAN equipment manufacturers would compete in carrier Central Office markets and vice versa; the PPSN concept partially materialized as MPLS and Metro Ethernet but was dominated by Cisco/Lucent, not Cabletron. The conceptual framework anticipated carrier-edge convergence.

Broadband access technology; Cabletron developing ADSL capability with Motorola; ADSL became mainstream broadband access technology in 2000s; now being replaced by fiber/VDSL2/cable; still deployed in some geographies.

Ipsilon's technology combining IP routing with ATM switching hardware; 5x router performance at fraction of cost; achieved via IFMP (Ipsilon Flow Management Protocol) and GSMP (Generic Switch Management Protocol); lost the standards battle to Cisco's MPLS/Tag Switching; IETF declined to standardize IFMP.

Cabletron's proprietary VLAN and policy-based network management framework; key features: connection-oriented switching, time-of-day/day-of-week access control, call-level accounting; SecureFast VLAN Operational Model published as RFC 2643 (August 1999); technology became obsolete with Cabletron's dissolution.
Protocol for managing ATM switches from IP routing layer; developed by Ipsilon; submitted to IETF; GSMP v3 eventually published as RFC 3292; technology path was superseded by MPLS.
Ipsilon's proprietary flow management protocol; core of IP Switching implementation; submitted to IETF but not adopted as standard; lost to Cisco's Tag Switching (MPLS precursor).

Cabletron's RFC 2124 protocol for policy management and accounting integration across switching platforms; one of several pre-MPLS flow management protocols that did not survive standardization process.

Cisco's competing approach to IP/ATM integration; submitted to IETF; became the basis for MPLS (Multi-Protocol Label Switching, RFC 3031); Tag Switching itself obsolete but MPLS remains fundamental to modern backbone networks.

The technology that ultimately won the IP/ATM integration standards battle; MPLS RFC 3031 published January 2001; derived from Cisco's Tag Switching; remains fundamental to carrier backbone networks, VPNs, and traffic engineering as of 2026.

Cabletron's ASIC-based switching hardware supporting VLAN, broadcast/multicast storm containment, Layer 3 virtual routing; available as modules for MMAC/MMAC-Plus chassis and standalone workgroup units; product line dissolved with Cabletron reorganization.

Aberdeen's umbrella term for front-office business applications; the category was subsequently renamed CRM (Customer Relationship Management) by Siebel, Gartner, and the analyst community; CIS as a term is now obsolete though the underlying capabilities persist in modern CRM platforms.

Subset of CIS covering lead management, opportunity tracking, account management, and sales forecasting; Aberdeen identifies SFA as the most mature CIS module at time of study; remains a core module of all modern CRM platforms.

Enabling technology linking phone systems to CRM/help desk software; Aberdeen identifies CTI as a critical CIS enabler; the technology evolved into modern UCaaS and contact-center-as-a-service (CCaaS) platforms.

Shift from client-server to internet-based CIS deployment; Aberdeen dedicates a chapter to this transition; became the dominant architecture by 2002-2005; the web paradigm eventually gave way to SaaS (Salesforce model, 1999+).

Aberdeen identifies CIS-ERP integration as a 'rapidly emerging trend'; proved highly accurate — became a major driver of SAP CRM, Oracle Siebel integration, and eventually the ERP-CRM convergence in cloud suites.
Mid-market SFA product; passed through Interact Commerce, Sage, Swiftpage; rebranded as Infor CRM then Act! Premium for Web; significantly diminished market position.
Industry-leading CRM suite; dominated market by 2000; acquired by Oracle (2005); Oracle Siebel CRM remains as on-premises legacy product; Oracle Fusion/CX is successor.
Help desk/ITSM workflow platform; Remedy ARS became BMC Remedy ITSM after 2002 acquisition; still actively used in enterprise environments as BMC Helix ITSM.

Not in study (founded 1999, post-report); included for context as the disruptive force that reshaped the entire CIS/CRM market Aberdeen was analyzing. Salesforce's SaaS model eventually displaced most vendors profiled in this report.

The de facto standard internetworking operating system; described by Aberdeen as 'unifying thread' providing cohesion to disparate networks; four cornerstones: scalability, WAN optimization, management/security, reliable adaptive routing; IOS remained dominant through 2000s; evolved into IOS XE, IOS XR for modern platforms; still the basis for Cisco's networking OS.

Cisco's per-flow packet forwarding and statistics technology; implemented in IOS 11 (1996); establishes flow cache to speed packet processing; exports per-conversation statistics; provides QoS and accounting capabilities; Aberdeen notes 10% processing overhead tradeoff; NetFlow v9 became basis for IETF IPFIX standard (RFC 7011, 2013); still widely used for network monitoring as of 2026.

Cisco's proprietary approach to combining Layer 2 switching with Layer 3 routing using simple tags; submitted to IETF; evolved into MPLS (Multi-Protocol Label Switching); RFC 3031 published 2001; Tag Switching itself replaced by MPLS standard but was the direct precursor.

The evolved and standardized version of Cisco's Tag Switching; IETF RFC 3031 (January 2001); fundamental to carrier backbone networks, MPLS VPNs, traffic engineering; still essential to carrier infrastructure as of 2026.

Cisco's next-generation router designed for ISPs and internet backbone; first deployed 1997; multigigabit crossbar fabric sustaining 80 Gbps; interfaces OC-3/OC-12/OC-48, Gigabit Ethernet, Packet over SONET, ATM; Aberdeen described as 'appropriate and necessary'; sold 1,000 units by November 1998; became a major commercial success; still referenced in carrier networks.

High-end ATM broadband switch from StrataCom acquisition; first integration point for Tag Switching and Cisco IOS; BXM Broadband Switching Modules based on Stratm Technology; key to Cisco's ISP market positioning; eventually superseded by IP/MPLS platforms.

High-performance ASIC set for ATM switching and traffic management from StrataCom; features complete ABR support, virtual path trunking, weighted fair queuing, multicast; enables higher-density lower-cost ATM switching; integrated into BPX platforms.

Aberdeen-era workhorse enterprise and service provider router; target for NetFlow and Tag Switching capabilities; VIP2 (Versatile Interface Processor 2) enhanced with high-speed interfaces; 4x capacity increase through new processors; eventually superseded by 7600, ASR 1000, and 12000 GSR series.

Enterprise internet commerce platform; components: transaction engine (Financial eXchange), application portfolio (Catalog, Digital Delivery, Chat, Note), Oasis distributed architecture, Workplace management tools; EDI integration driver; CORBA and DCOM neutral via abstraction layer; clients included Broderbund, MicroWarehouse, Multiple Zones, Comdisco, Scholastic.

tech_name: Oasis (Object Architecture and Scalable Interoperating System)
notes: InterWorld's distributed transaction processing platform; abstraction layer between applications and object architectures (CORBA/DCOM); avoids technology lock-in; remote object marshaling to minimize network communication; eliminates universal messaging queue bottlenecks; dissolved with InterWorld.

OMG standard for distributed object communication; Aberdeen reports Oracle and BroadVision promoting CORBA for enterprise internet commerce; InterWorld's Oasis neutral between CORBA and DCOM; CORBA largely superseded by REST, SOAP, and API-based integration by 2010.

Microsoft's distributed object model for Windows-based internet commerce; competing with CORBA for enterprise internet commerce standard; InterWorld's Oasis supports DCOM without translation gateways; DCOM largely obsolete, superseded by WCF, REST, and cloud APIs.

Electronic document interchange for B2B transactions (purchase orders, invoices); Fortune 1000 standard for supply chain integration; Commerce eXchange supports EDI via driver for B2B channel; still used in legacy supply chain systems but being replaced by API-based integration and EDI-over-AS2/AS4.

Aberdeen notes Commerce eXchange uses SSL to protect sessions between browser and server; RC4 and MD5 encryption; SSL deprecated in favor of TLS (Transport Layer Security); TLS 1.3 is current standard; SSL itself obsolete.

InterWorld's digital goods download component; compliant with Microsoft ESD guidelines and SPA EULA; integrates with software clearinghouses; precursor concept to modern app stores (App Store 2008, Steam, etc.).

The emerging category of software that Commerce eXchange exemplified; Aberdeen's analysis of this category anticipates modern e-commerce platforms (Shopify, Magento, Salesforce Commerce Cloud, etc.); the category grew to a multi-billion dollar market.
Commercial RISC/Unix market peaked mid-2000s; replaced by x86-64 Linux servers

DataBlade technology absorbed into IBM after Informix acquisition in 2001

OLTP remains a core enterprise workload category

N-tiered client-server CRM suite including Sales Enterprise and Service Enterprise; acquired by Oracle 2006; continues as Oracle Siebel CRM
Flagship SFA product; contact manager, activity manager, scheduler, calendar, EIS reporting; version 3.0 released February 1997
Customer support module; service request logging, escalation, contract management, online knowledge base
Multi-tier application architecture separating presentation, business logic, and data; became standard enterprise architecture pattern
Software automating sales processes; core market for Siebel; became part of broader CRM category
Broad category managing customer lifecycle; Siebel helped define and dominate this market
Integration of phone systems with CRM data; Siebel 3.0 integrated with Prospect and Genesys CTI middleware
Graphical configuration tool introduced in Siebel v3.0 allowing customization without source code modification
Siebel's development platform; contributed to Windows-centric deployment; MFC largely superseded by .NET
Siebel anticipated pen computing for field sales; evolved into mobile CRM on smartphones and tablets
ETI•EXTRACT was a pioneering commercial ETL tool; market eventually consolidated around Informatica, IBM DataStage
Data warehousing became mainstream; evolved into cloud data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift)
ETL became standard data integration pattern; evolved to ELT in cloud era
Metadata management became critical discipline; modern data catalogs (Collibra, Alation) are successors
COBOL programs generated by ETI•EXTRACT for mainframe targets; COBOL still runs significant legacy code as of 2026
SAP ABAP language supported by ETI; ABAP still active in SAP environments
IBM DB2 supported as ETI•EXTRACT target; DB2 still active as of 2026
IBM Visual Warehouse used ETI•EXTRACT for data extraction; discontinued and replaced by IBM Warehouse Manager
Y2K conversion completed by 2000; ETI•EXTRACT used as data migration tool during Y2K projects
Client/server migration from mainframe peaked late 1990s; superseded by web/cloud architectures
IBM RPG language supported by ETI•EXTRACT for AS/400 targets; RPG still used in IBM i environments
RDBMS remains dominant; cloud databases added columnar and multi-model variants
ETI MetaStore was OO database; OODB largely displaced by RDBMS with OO extensions and NoSQL
Data mining rebranded as machine learning and predictive analytics; now dominant enterprise discipline
Decision trees remain widely used; Random Forest and Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) are major extensions
Neural networks became deep learning; transformer models and LLMs are current dominant form (2023+)
Association rules (Apriori algorithm) still used in recommendation systems and market basket analysis
MMP evolved; modern distributed computing (Spark, Ray) is the successor architecture
Parallelism became standard in ML training; GPU computing (NVIDIA CUDA) now primary parallel compute for ML
Commercial data mining platforms evolved into AutoML and cloud ML services (AWS SageMaker, Azure ML, GCP Vertex)
Predictive analytics became standard business practice across financial services, retail, telecom
K-means and other clustering algorithms remain widely used; deep learning-based clustering extended
Customer behavior/churn analysis became standard; powers subscription businesses and e-commerce globally
NuTCRACKER v1.0 1994; v3.1 May 1997; continued under MKS; eventually superseded by containerization and cloud migration
NT evolved into Windows 2000 and Windows Server line; NT kernel is basis of all modern Windows
Win32 still exists but modern Windows development uses .NET and UWP; Win32 legacy maintained
POSIX standard still important; NT's POSIX subsystem deprecated; WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux) is modern equivalent
DirectLink enabled Unix RDBMS library access on NT; discontinued with NuTCRACKER under PTC
Unix RISC workstations displaced by x86 Windows and Linux workstations by mid-2000s
MKS Toolkit provided Unix shell and utilities on Windows; included with NuTCRACKER; now legacy
X Windows/Motif was standard Unix GUI; Wintif in NuTCRACKER converted Motif to native Windows UI
Pentium processors cited as NuTCRACKER target; x86 architecture became dominant through x86-64
DCOM supported by NuTCRACKER DirectLink; DCOM evolved into .NET Remoting then WCF then REST
Cross-platform porting evolved into containerization (Docker) and cloud-native development
Tuxedo libraries supported via DirectLink; Oracle Tuxedo still active in legacy environments
Joint product combining EMC intelligent storage with BMC data movement software; bypassed mainframe scheduling constraints
Extracting data from mainframe systems (IBM z/OS) for use in open systems environments; still practiced in large enterprises
Central repository for integrated data supporting decision making; term popularized by Bill Inmon in early 1990s
Subject-specific subsets of data warehouses for departmental or functional use
Unix and Windows-based computing environments contrasted with proprietary mainframe systems; now largely cloud and x86 Linux
LinkWorks discontinued after DEC-Compaq-HP mergers; replaced by SharePoint and other ECM platforms
WebWorker was web gateway for LinkWorks; discontinued with LinkWorks itself
CORBA widely adopted in late 1990s but superseded by web services (SOAP, REST) by 2005

Runs all apps on host CPU with only keystrokes/mouse transmitted to remote; bandwidth-efficient for dial-up; LapLink's core architecture
Client-server approach built into Windows 95/NT Dial-Up Networking; suited for small file transfers/email not bandwidth-intensive apps
Proprietary file synchronization transferring only changed file portions; reduces transfer times by up to 80%
Most prevalent connectivity for telecommuters in 1997; modem-based dial-up; phased out as broadband adoption grew post-2000
Microsoft's built-in remote desktop solution (introduced in Windows NT 4.0 Terminal Server 1998); eventually superseded LapLink remote control
Encrypted tunneling solution that became dominant remote access approach post-2000; supplanted LapLink dial-up remote control
First scalable carrier-grade VoIP gateway; 24+ ports; POTS-to-POTS capability; Aberdeen's product of focus
Core enabling technology; routing voice and fax over IP networks; became standard for all voice communications by 2010s
elemedia's proprietary voice codec delivering near toll-grade sound quality; adopted by Netscape Media Server
Carrier network protocol enabling internet telephony; rapidly expanding in service provider networks
Traditional circuit-switched telephone network; Lucent's solution enables POTS-to-POTS calling over IP without user behavior change
Eventual dominant VoIP signaling protocol (RFC 2543 1999); superseded proprietary gateway approaches like Lucent's initial implementation
Early VoIP signaling standard; competed with SIP; eventually lost to SIP for most applications
QoS mechanism for managing bandwidth; relevant for internet telephony quality of service guarantees

Xedia's managed broadband access device; combines high-speed IP routing with Class Based Queuing bandwidth management; first release supports Frame Relay; 50% below cost of competing connectivity-only solutions
Bandwidth management technique organizing traffic into classes with different priority/allocation; Xedia's Access Point implements CBQ; later became standard in Cisco IOS and Linux
Xedia's proprietary QoS software allowing IT managers to assign bandwidth priorities and allocations; deep buffering to cut congestion/latency
Most widely used WAN transmission uplink technology cited by Aberdeen; Access Point first release supports Frame Relay; declined as MPLS and broadband alternatives grew
Internet protocol cited for its inability to prioritize applications or guarantee bandwidth; core limitation requiring bandwidth management overlay
Subsequent Access Point release planned to support ATM; ATM itself largely abandoned by mid-2000s as IP/Ethernet dominated
Technology that superseded Frame Relay for enterprise WAN; standardized 1999; became dominant enterprise WAN transport
Modern evolution of bandwidth management problem Aberdeen identified in 1997; SD-WAN directly addresses dynamic bandwidth allocation Aberdeen described

tech_name: Enterprise Training and Change Management Platforms
notes: Aberdeen identified growing market for customized, on-demand training delivery beyond classroom instruction; foreshadowed LMS and e-learning explosion.
Respondents demanded 'on-demand, on-line, real-time, continuous access' training — directly describing what became e-learning platforms.
Study documents emerging field of structured change management as a project discipline, preceding Kotter and ADKAR formalization.
Implied major technology transition category; ERP rollouts were the quintessential challenge context for this research.
Hardware-based fault-tolerant NT server; ~$24,995; two lock-stepped Pentium Pro CEs + two IOPs; 99.99% uptime; 1.5km fiber separation. Superseded by software-based everRun in 2004.
NT Server 4.0 released July 1996; growing enterprise adoption but lacked built-in fault tolerance. Superseded by Windows Server 2000/2003/2008 line.

Wolfpack shipped as MSCS with NT Server 4.0 Enterprise Edition in Sept 1997; provided failover clustering with minutes-of-downtime recovery. Renamed Windows Server Failover Clustering in Windows Server 2008.
Marathon connected Endurance 4000 at the HAL level — below normal program accessibility — to achieve transparent fault tolerance.
Software-only successor to Endurance 4000 hardware; released 2004 for Windows Server 2003; acquired by Stratus 2012.
The Endurance 4000 required matched uni-processor Pentium Pro servers as Computing Elements.
Logan argues NT Server lacks production-quality enterprise credentials; no enterprises including Microsoft itself running mission-critical on it.
BackOffice positioned as mainframe/Unix replacement; Logan argues it lacks enterprise IS planning/support/management culture.
One of the enterprise platforms Logan contrasts with Microsoft; remains in production use in banking, insurance, government.
Enterprise-grade Unix platforms that Logan contrasts with Microsoft's desktop-oriented products.
Named as production-quality enterprise platform that Microsoft products are positioned to replace.
Kastner's Aberdeen had called NT Server 4.0 'a giant step forward' in July 1996; this study questions enterprise enterprise-class readiness.
BackOffice suite (Exchange, SQL Server, SNA Server, Systems Management Server) central to Microsoft's enterprise pitch. Discontinued as bundle ca. 2002.
Scalability Day claimed 1.8M emails/day with 50,000 users/server; but Kastner noted this was not yet shipping and customers reported scaling beyond a few hundred users was problematic.
Demonstrated 1 billion transactions/day and terabyte satellite imagery; Kastner critiqued benchmarks as unrealistic and TPC-A based.
100M internet hits/day benchmark on single 2-processor Pentium Pro with NT; Kastner challenged realism of 24-hour even load distribution.
Kastner's assessment: 'real enterprise high-end legitimately remains the province of Unix, the AS/400, and even the mainframe.'
Named alongside Unix and mainframe as legitimately retaining the enterprise high-end; still sold as IBM i today.
SQL Server VLM support announced as future feature; Oracle and Informix already supported on Digital Unix/Alpha for over a year.
Central subject of migration study. Aberdeen predicts end-of-support within 18 months of 1997. Long since discontinued.
Successor to Sybase System 10. Lacked row-level locking at time of study. Evolved into Adaptive Server Enterprise (ASE). Retired after SAP acquisition.
Core Informix RDBMS product. Strong SMP scalability. Became IBM Informix after 2001 acquisition. Actively maintained by HCLSoftware since 2017.
Combines Informix-OnLine with Illustra DataBlade object-relational extensions. Evolved into IBM Informix IDS.
Extended Informix parallel scalability to clustered/MPP environments. Version 8.0. Later superseded by IBM Informix IDS with XPS.
Object-oriented rapid application development toolkit for Informix. Version 2.0 featured building-block class libraries.
Informix extensibility mechanism for adding complex data types (text, spatial, video, time series, ROLAP). 25 modules available at time of study.
Key differentiator for Informix vs. Sybase System 11. Aberdeen highlights SMP as critical for data marts and web servers.
Sybase System 11 lacked row-level locking at time of study, preventing SAP support. Informix-OnLine supported it. Row-level locking is now a universal standard feature.
Target language for migration from Sybase Transact-SQL stored procedures via Informix's stored procedure parser.
Sybase's proprietary SQL extension. Also licensed to Microsoft as T-SQL for SQL Server.
Client connectivity API for Sybase. Informix promised a converter (2Q97) to allow 4.9 clients to run against Informix transparently.
Central platform of study; replaced by Windows 2000 Server in 2000; line continues as Windows Server 2003/2008/2012/2016/2019/2022/2025
NCR's high-availability transaction processing NT server line; Aberdeen endorses for enterprise HATP; NCR exited commodity server market circa 2000-2002
Compaq ProLiant NT server line; competitor to NCR; Compaq acquired by HP 2002; ProLiant brand absorbed into HP ProLiant then HPE ProLiant
HP NetServer NT line; competitor; evolved into HPE ProLiant post-2015 split
IBM Netfinity NT server line; competitor; IBM sold x86 server business to Lenovo in 2005 for $1.75B
Server-independent file-level storage over Ethernet (NFS/SMB). NAS devices began gaining popularity mid-1990s. Now a mainstream enterprise and cloud storage paradigm.
High-performance block-level storage network using Fibre Channel. Enterprise SAN market exploded in the late 1990s/2000s.
Traditional server-centric storage. The 'decades-old architecture' Aberdeen targets as becoming inadequate.
Primary SAN interconnect protocol in late 1990s. Still used in high-performance enterprise environments.
Core UNIX NAS protocol. Sun Microsystems introduced NFS in 1984; became standard by the mid-1990s.
Central to Nortel's multimedia vision. ATM was eventually displaced by Ethernet/MPLS/IP for enterprise networking. Nortel's ATM focus was a strategic risk.
Nortel's flagship ATM switching product line. Claimed #1 in multimedia networking. Passport enabled multimedia voice/data/video; documented customer savings of $435,000/year.
X.25 and TDM packet switches. Popular in traditional WAN deployments.
Nortel's flagship PBX product line for enterprise voice. Became Avaya product after 2009 asset sale.
Standard WAN protocol at time of study. Customer testimonial involved 10-county frame relay network.
Referenced as a WAN service option. VPN became a mainstream enterprise technology.
ITU standard for videoconferencing over ISDN. Nortel offered H.320 network applications. Superseded by IP-based video (H.323, SIP, WebRTC).
Integration of telephony with computing. Nortel highlighted CTI as a differentiator. CTI became ubiquitous in call centers.
Fundamental enterprise networking concept discussed throughout study.
Referenced as providing survivability for six-sigma reliability in Nortel ATM networks.
Hierarchical enterprise directory. Introduced NetWare 4 (1993). Ported to NT/Unix 1997-1998. Rebranded eDirectory ~2000. Acquired chain led to OpenText ownership.
Microsoft directory service for Windows 2000. Aberdeen predicted release mid-1998; actually shipped February 2000. Became dominant enterprise directory.
Standards-based directory access protocol. LDAPv3 approved December 1997 as proposed Internet standard. Now universal directory protocol.
OSI-based directory standard. Basis for NDS and AD conceptual models. Largely superseded by LDAP for practical deployments.
NetWare NOS with Internet/intranet capabilities. Novell's core product. Succeeded by NetWare 5/6 and eventually Open Enterprise Server on SUSE Linux.
Dominant enterprise NOS through mid-1990s. Lost market share to Windows NT and Windows 2000. Formally discontinued 2009.
Email and groupware suite built on NDS. Competed with Lotus Notes and Microsoft Exchange. Still marketed by OpenText.
LAN management product integrated with NDS. Integration with CA Unicenter TNG announced. Discontinued early 2000s.
CA's enterprise management suite. Integrated with ManageWise for WAN coverage. Evolved into CA Technologies management portfolio.
Directory integration with VPN identified as key infrastructure need. VPN technology became standard enterprise connectivity.
NDS-based application deployment tool allowing remote software push to workstations. Predecessor to modern endpoint management.
Banyan's enterprise directory service. Competitor to NDS. Declined with Banyan's enterprise business.

Deployed as sole OS for LAN server, application server, database server, and communications server at the Company. NT 4.0 was current version. Significant scalability and stability problems documented.
Deployed for developer and call center operator desktops. Most stable workstations required 2-3 reboots per day at time of study.
Replaced by NT mandate. OS/2 Warp 3 for call center servers; OS/2 Warp 4 for development; OS/2 Connect for workstations. Described as very stable before migration.

Deployed in call center operations. Documented: database crashes requiring hours/days to rebuild, frequent corruption, poor cache management, inadequate referential integrity and replication. Lacked row-level locking.
Deployed for mobile users under the 1995 Microsoft mandate.
The Company was a multi-platform DCE environment. SQL Server's single-platform support was a documented concern.
ODMG-compliant object database. Acquired by Informix 1998; absorbed into IBM portfolio 2001. Technology lineage ended.
ODMG-93 to ODMG 3.0 (2000). Disbanded 2001 when members shifted focus to Java Data Objects (JDO). Never achieved mainstream adoption.
Pure object databases remained niche market. By 1999 IDC reported $211M OODBMS revenue vs $11.1B relational/object-relational. Still used in specialized domains.
Remained dominant. IDC 1999: $11.1B RDBMS revenue. Annual growth rate 18.2% through 2004 vs 12.5% for OODBMS.
ODMG-standard SQL-like query language for object databases. Superseded by SQL3/SQL:1999 object-relational extensions and JDO.

Synchronized demand management solution with object architecture. Aberdeen praised its design as capable of leading next technology phase. Market fate unknown; demand management consolidated into SAP Oracle and i2 Technologies.

Object-oriented programming as applied to enterprise applications. Became dominant paradigm through Java and C++ in enterprise software. Aberdeen identifies as key to adaptable core business processes.
Aberdeen's term for integrated enterprise applications supporting inter-business operations. Corresponds to what became ERP/SCM/CRM suites from SAP Oracle and others.
Aberdeen Fortune-class clients moving aggressively to adopt client-server as production standard. Succeeded by web/cloud architectures in 2000s.
Demand chain management identified as key application area for OOT. SCM software market grew to multi-billion dollar segment dominated by SAP i2 Manugistics and Oracle.
Aberdeen's concept of electronically-enabled virtual enterprises via object technology. Realized through web services SOA microservices and B2B integration platforms.

Single-sideband FM technique for two-way broadcast-quality video over twisted pair phone wire. Product configuration: PBX switch (5-100 users) + modem-sized desktop device. Below $3000/user. Appears obsolete; company did not survive.
Existing phone line used as VidModem transport. VidModem key value proposition: no rewiring required. Same infrastructure later used for DSL broadband.
VidModem interfaced with ATM as wide area transport. Telcos promoting ATM backed VidModem. ATM itself declined as IP/MPLS and Ethernet WAN superseded it.
Aberdeen's reference case for telco deployment risk: ISDN applications died waiting for telco buildout. VidModem faced same risk. ISDN largely obsolete by 2010.
IP-based video conferencing standard. Aberdeen does not mention H.323 (ratified 1996) — competitor to VidModem approach. H.323 ultimately displaced analog FM video.
Aberdeen coins 'CTVI' (Computer Telephony Video Integration) for VidModem's seamless computer-phone-video integration. CTI became standard call center technology without VidModem.
Proprietary analog technique enabling NTSC PAL S-VHS broadcast quality video over single twisted pair alongside voice. Novel analog approach in an increasingly digital world.
IP-based browser-native video conferencing that ultimately solved the problem VidModem attempted. Zoom Teams Meet built on WebRTC/SIP foundations.
VidModem switch installed next to PBX. No rewiring required. PBX itself gradually displaced by VoIP/UCaaS platforms.

name: Digital LinkWorks
lifecycle_note: Discontinued after DEC was acquired by Compaq (1998); product not carried forward under HP.

name: Lotus Notes
lifecycle_note: Acquired by IBM 1995; evolved through IBM Lotus Notes to HCL Notes after HCL Technologies acquired the product line from IBM in 2019.

name: Microsoft Exchange
lifecycle_note: Still actively developed and widely deployed as of 2026; available as Exchange Server and Exchange Online (Microsoft 365).

name: OLE (Object Linking and Embedding)
lifecycle_note: OLE evolved into COM/DCOM and ActiveX; concepts persist in modern Windows development and COM automation.

name: Distributed Object Technology / CORBA
lifecycle_note: Superseded by web services (SOAP/REST), SOA, and microservices architectures; OMG CORBA standard technically maintained but rarely used in new development.

name: Digital UNIX (Tru64)
lifecycle_note: Renamed Tru64 UNIX under Compaq; end-of-life support ended by HP around 2012.

name: HP-UX
lifecycle_note: HP-UX still supported as of 2026 for Integrity servers; Hewlett Packard Enterprise announced end of hardware support for Itanium in 2025.

name: OpenVMS
lifecycle_note: VMS Software Inc. (VSI) acquired OpenVMS from HP in 2014 and continues active development as of 2026.

name: IBM AIX
lifecycle_note: IBM AIX remains actively supported as of 2026 for IBM Power Systems.

name: Windows NT
lifecycle_note: Windows NT 3.5 end of life 2001; NT kernel architecture evolved through Windows 2000, XP, and into all modern Windows versions.

name: Windows 3.1 / Windows for Workgroups
lifecycle_note: End of life December 2001; superseded by Windows 95, 98, and later Windows XP.

name: Digital All-in-1
lifecycle_note: DEC's All-in-1 office automation suite discontinued after DEC acquisition by Compaq. No modern successor.

name: Oracle Database
lifecycle_note: Oracle Database still actively developed and deployed as of 2026 (Oracle 23c).

name: Microsoft SQL Server
lifecycle_note: Microsoft SQL Server still actively developed and deployed as of 2026.

name: RPC (Remote Procedure Call)
lifecycle_note: RPC concept evolved into web services, gRPC, and REST APIs; DCE RPC itself largely obsolete in new development.

name: OS/2 Presentation Manager
lifecycle_note: IBM discontinued mainstream OS/2 support in 2006; ArcaOS (Arca Noae) provides continued support for legacy deployments.

name: Motif (X Window GUI)

description: Software layer acting as a traffic cop directing requests between distributed client and server systems; maintains a directory/repository of objects and their interfaces.
lifecycle_notes: ORB/CORBA-based middleware largely supplanted by web services (SOAP/REST), ESBs, and microservices architectures. Still used in legacy enterprise environments.

description: Digital Equipment Corporation's ORB implementation; version 2.5 at time of study. Supports 19 hardware/OS combinations. Evolved from ACA Services offering.
lifecycle_notes: Product discontinued following Digital's acquisition by Compaq in 1998. Superseded by DCOM/COM+ and later web services.

description: OMG standard for distributed object interoperability using Interface Definition Language (IDL). ObjectBroker conforms to CORBA 1.2 at time of study.
lifecycle_notes: CORBA remains technically active in some sectors (finance, defense, telecoms via IONA/Micro Focus) but is broadly considered legacy middleware displaced by REST/SOAP/gRPC.

description: Language-neutral specification language used to define object interfaces in CORBA. ObjectBroker 2.5 supports IDL bindings to C and a Visual Basic DLL.
lifecycle_notes: IDL persists within CORBA/OMG standards and in some API description tools, but mainstream use is supplanted by interface description formats like OpenAPI and Protocol Buffers.

description: Digital extension to IDL describing characteristics of each object implementation.
lifecycle_notes: Proprietary Digital extension; discontinued with ObjectBroker product.

description: Digital extension to IDL specifying how to select among multiple implementations of an object.
lifecycle_notes: Proprietary Digital extension; discontinued with ObjectBroker product.

description: Microsoft standard for compound documents and inter-application communication. ObjectBroker 2.5 provides OLE Network Portal for OLE-CORBA integration.
lifecycle_notes: OLE evolved into OLE 2.0, then ActiveX/COM, then COM+, and ultimately .NET component model. Legacy OLE automation still used in some Office integration scenarios.

description: Microsoft binary interface standard underlying OLE. Defines how software components interact. CORBA and COM coexist in ObjectBroker strategy.
lifecycle_notes: COM evolved into COM+/.NET. COM interop still supported in .NET but COM itself is legacy for new development. Windows Runtime (WinRT) is the modern successor.

description: Older Microsoft inter-process communication protocol. ObjectBroker 2.5 includes a DDE Gateway/Listener for legacy DDE-aware servers.
lifecycle_notes: DDE largely deprecated since Windows 2000 era; succeeded by COM/OLE and DCOM. Some legacy automation tools still use it.

description: OSF/DCE standard for distributed computing security (GSS API). ObjectBroker 2.6 roadmap includes GSS API for DCE to enable Kerberos-level security.
lifecycle_notes: DCE largely obsolete; superseded by Kerberos directly, LDAP, and modern identity/auth frameworks such as OAuth2 and OpenID Connect.

description: Microsoft RAD tool mentioned as a front-end application development option for ORB-based architectures. ObjectBroker 2.5 supports a Visual Basic DLL IDL binding.
lifecycle_notes: Visual Basic evolved into VB.NET (introduced 2002 with .NET Framework). Classic VB 6.0 development ended; VB.NET remains supported but is niche relative to C#.

description: Sybase (then Appeon) RAD development tool mentioned as a front-end option for building OLE-aware client applications against CORBA backends.
lifecycle_notes: PowerBuilder persists in niche enterprise markets under Appeon ownership (acquired from SAP in 2014); new development is minimal but legacy applications remain.

description: Digital's VMS operating system for both VAX and Alpha hardware. Supported by ObjectBroker on both architectures.
lifecycle_notes: OpenVMS still supported under VSI (VMS Software Inc), which licensed OpenVMS from HP (later HPE) and continues active development for x86-64 in addition to Alpha and Integrity.

description: CORBA 2.0 IDL-to-C++ binding planned for ObjectBroker 2.6; would allow native C++ development environment.
lifecycle_notes: C++ remains an active systems programming language; CORBA C++ binding (OMG IDL-to-C++) is legacy but C++ itself is widely used.

description: Standard API for security services. ObjectBroker 2.6 roadmap includes GSS API for DCE enabling Kerberos-level authentication.
lifecycle_notes: GSS-API/Kerberos remains active and widely deployed (Active Directory, MIT Kerberos, Heimdal). The API standard itself is still relevant.

description: Digital's predecessor distributed application integration offering that evolved into ObjectBroker.
lifecycle_notes: ACA Services was an earlier Digital product superseded by ObjectBroker. Both now obsolete.

description: Digital's modified version of Protosoft's Paradigm Plus, customized to automatically generate IDL code from object-oriented analysis and design models.
lifecycle_notes: ObjectPlus was a co-developed tool with Protosoft; Protosoft acquired by Platinum Technology in November 1995, shortly after this study. ObjectPlus discontinued.

description: Protosoft's object-oriented analysis and design (OOA/D) tool, basis for Digital's ObjectPlus. Acquired by Platinum Technology in November 1995.
lifecycle_notes: Paradigm Plus acquired by Platinum Technology (Nov 1995) which was itself acquired by Computer Associates (1999). Product subsequently discontinued.

description: Digital's Network Application Support platform installed on over half of Digital systems sold. ObjectBroker runtime included as part of NAS.
lifecycle_notes: NAS was a Digital platform-level offering discontinued with Digital's acquisition by Compaq in 1998.

notes: Umbrella term for client-server apps for customer acquisition and retention; includes customer support, helpdesk, telemarketing, SFA
Integration of telephone systems with information systems; key enabling technology for CIS; evolved into modern contact center platforms
HP's comprehensive CIS program unveiled December 1995; combines software, partnerships, hardware; no longer marketed under this brand
HP CTI middleware; application- and communication-device-independent; runs on top of ACT or TSAPI; priced $300-$800 per user
Device-specific CTI link between PBX switch and information systems network; deployed at 200+ call center sites by Dec 1995
Emerging call control standard; CCM planned Q1 1996 support; grew but eventually superseded by TAPI and SIP
CTI middleware supporting Alcatel, Ericsson, and Rolm switches; HP CCM interfaces with this product
Telephone switch system; core hardware in call centers; still widely deployed
Automated call routing and distribution; core call center infrastructure; still active and evolved
Automated voice response systems; listed as category of CIS ISV partners; still active but evolved
Software for automating sales processes; key CIS application category; evolved into modern CRM
Customer support and helpdesk ticketing applications; key CIS category; evolved into ITSM and modern helpdesk platforms
Process automation software for CIS; listed as partner category; evolved into modern BPM platforms
Business intelligence and data warehousing for CIS; HP OpenWarehouse program; evolved into modern analytics
HP's Unix OS; enhanced with symmetric multiprocessing; leading commercial RISC/Unix server platform in 1995
HP leading supplier of commercial RISC/Unix servers; HP K100 to T-520 (12-way, 5621 tpm-C)
Network management software; used for call center network management in CIS deployments
HP email platform; provides communication capabilities to CSRs and administrators
HP data warehousing program; provides additional data analytics for CIS deployments
Caller identification technology enabling screen pop-up in CIS; standard call center feature
CTI feature delivering caller data to agent screen automatically on call arrival; key CCM capability
Deployment architecture for CIS; replacing mainframe/home-grown systems in Fortune 1000; evolved into web/cloud
Legacy mainframe terminal interface; CCM surrounds existing 3270 systems enabling migration path
AI-assisted problem resolution for customer support; Carnegie Group in Figure 2; evolved into knowledge management
Video capability planned for CIS deployments; InSoft in Figure 2; evolved into modern unified communications

name: Graphical Class Editor
lifecycle_note: SoftBench 5.0 feature; discontinued with parent product

name: SoftBench Encapsulator

name: Sub-Process Control Daemon (SPCD)
lifecycle_note: SoftBench cross-platform development daemon; discontinued with SoftBench

name: HP Object-Oriented DCE (OODCE)
lifecycle_note: HP product for building DCE-based OO applications; discontinued

name: HP Distributed Smalltalk (DST)
lifecycle_note: HP Smalltalk CORBA implementation; discontinued

name: HP ORB Plus
lifecycle_note: HP CORBA ORB for C++ announced for 1996 release; discontinued

name: CommonPoint

name: HP-UX
lifecycle_note: HP's Unix OS; HPE announced end-of-life for HP-UX 11i v3 December 31, 2025

name: Sun Solaris
lifecycle_note: Sun Microsystems OS; Oracle now owns Solaris; Oracle Solaris 11.4 in extended support

name: C++ Language
lifecycle_note: ISO-standardized language; C++23 published; widely used as of 2026

name: COBOL
lifecycle_note: Still used extensively in mainframe/legacy banking and government systems as of 2026

name: Micro Focus COBOL
lifecycle_note: COBOL tooling referenced in study; now under OpenText (acquired Micro Focus 2023)

name: OSF Distributed Computing Environment (DCE)
lifecycle_note: OSF/Open Group standard for distributed computing; largely replaced by web services and REST; technically maintained by The Open Group but rarely deployed

name: CORBA
lifecycle_note: OMG standard for distributed OO; superseded by web services and REST in most contexts; limited legacy use persists

name: UIM/X
lifecycle_note: Motif-based GUI builder used in HP deployment scenarios; discontinued

name: XEmacs Editor
lifecycle_note: Open-source Emacs fork; still available as of 2026 though less dominant than GNU Emacs

name: Common Desktop Environment (CDE)
lifecycle_note: Unix desktop environment; open-sourced in 2012; now CDE on SourceForge; rarely used in production

name: HP VUE (Visual User Environment)
lifecycle_note: HP's desktop environment that preceded CDE; discontinued

name: Informix Database
lifecycle_note: Supported database in SoftBench 5.0; IBM acquired Informix 2001; IBM ended Informix continuing support April 2025

name: Oracle Database
lifecycle_note: Supported database in SoftBench 5.0; Oracle DB still widely deployed as of 2026

name: Sybase
lifecycle_note: Supported database in SoftBench 5.0; acquired by SAP in 2010; SAP ASE successor still available

name: DB2/9000
lifecycle_note: HP-UX port of IBM DB2; discontinued with HP-UX product line

name: CICS
lifecycle_note: IBM transaction processing middleware; still actively used in mainframe environments as of 2026

notes: Introduced May 1994; PowerPC AS-based models announced September 1995 for availability before end of 1995
status_note: Renamed iSeries in 2000, then System i in 2006, then IBM i in 2008; platform still active

notes: 64-bit RISC microprocessor at heart of new AS/400 Advanced Series models; also known as PowerPC AS
status_note: PowerPC AS evolved into the POWER architecture series; POWER still active

notes: Introduced to exploit 64-bit RISC architecture; required platform for SAP R/3 v3.0 on AS/400; implements Single Unix Specification (Spec 1170)
status_note: OS/400 renamed IBM i OS in 2008; still active

notes: Three-tier client-server ERP suite; version 3.0 announced for AS/400 September 1995; introduced 1992
status_note: SAP R/3 replaced by SAP ERP (ECC) then SAP S/4HANA; R/3 support ended 2015

notes: SAP's mainframe-based predecessor product; still part of overall SAP strategy in 1995 but eclipsed by R/3
status_note: SAP R/2 mainframe product eventually sunset

notes: Integrated RDBMS on AS/400; ability to run SAP R/3 as 64-bit application supported by 64-bit DB2/400 cited as key advantage
status_note: DB2/400 still active as Db2 for i on IBM i platform

notes: SAP's proprietary 4GL development language and workbench; used to develop all R/3 applications; provides database independence layer

notes: SAP R/3 deployed in three-tier client-server architecture with presentation/application/database separation

tech_name: R/3 Repository and SAP Information Model

SAP R/3 presentation layer supports Windows 3.1, Windows NT, and OS/2 GUIs simultaneously

name: DB2 Common Server
lifecycle_note: Continues as IBM Db2; current versions available for cloud and on-premises

name: DB2 Parallel Edition
lifecycle_note: Capabilities absorbed into mainstream IBM Db2

name: SQL

name: DRDA (Distributed Relational Database Architecture)
lifecycle_note: DRDA largely superseded by modern REST APIs and JDBC/ODBC connectivity; still used in some IBM mainframe environments

name: ODBC
lifecycle_note: ODBC remains a widely used database connectivity standard

name: X/Open CLI
lifecycle_note: X/Open CLI largely superseded by JDBC and modern ORMs; ODBC descended from it

name: DataPropagator
lifecycle_note: DataPropagator functionality absorbed into IBM InfoSphere Data Replication and IBM Data Replication products

name: DataJoiner
lifecycle_note: DataJoiner functionality absorbed into IBM InfoSphere Federation Server

name: DataHub
lifecycle_note: DataHub replaced by IBM Data Management Console and other tools

name: DataRefresher
lifecycle_note: Superseded by modern ETL tools

name: DB2 Visualizer
lifecycle_note: Superseded by modern BI tools

name: DataBasic (Stored Procedure Builder)
lifecycle_note: Superseded by modern DB2 development tools

name: VisualAge
lifecycle_note: VisualAge superseded by IBM Rational and Eclipse-based tools

name: OS/2 Warp
lifecycle_note: OS/2 discontinued; IBM ended OS/2 support in December 2006

name: AIX
lifecycle_note: AIX remains IBM's enterprise Unix OS for Power Systems

name: Windows NT
lifecycle_note: Windows NT superseded by Windows 2000 and later Windows Server versions

name: IBM RS/6000
lifecycle_note: RS/6000 line evolved into IBM Power Systems

name: IBM RS/6000 SP
lifecycle_note: RS/6000 SP evolved into IBM Blue Gene and later HPC platforms

name: TCP/IP
lifecycle_note: TCP/IP remains the dominant networking protocol

name: SNA LU6.2 (APPC/APPN)
lifecycle_note: SNA largely replaced by TCP/IP in enterprise networks

name: CICS
lifecycle_note: CICS remains active as IBM CICS Transaction Server

name: Encina
